en: Hello, my favorite English language learners! It is your favorite English teacher here, Amy Joy,
and today I have two big announcements to make. Number one, I'm starting to write an e-book!
And when I asked you all what you wanted my e-book to be about, most of you said C1 and C2
level vocabulary. So if you feel stuck at B1, B2 level vocabulary, or you're taking the IELTS or
TOEFL soon, or you just want to expand your vocabulary, then make sure you sign up for my
email list at the link below so that you're one of the first ones to find out when my new e-book
launches. And this leads me to my second announcement. In this video, you are going
to learn 15 super useful C1 and C2 vocabulary words. And in order to practice, I have created
a free downloadable PDF for you that will be very similar to the activities in my e-book.
So if you want to get a little preview of what my e-book is going to like, or you just want to
practice using the vocabulary from this video, go ahead, click the link below, and download the free
PDF. And since this is an example of what the e-book will look like, if you have any feedback
or ideas about what you would like to see in the e-book, let me know. I'm making this for you all
and I really hope you like it. So with that being said, let's get started. The title of our reading
today is AI in Journalism, a Revolutionary Innovation or a Clash of Values. Let's take a
look at the words AI, revolutionary, and clash. AI is an abbreviation for artificial intelligence,
which is the studying or making of computers that can copy human behavior. And some sample
sentences with this word include, the development of AI has revolutionized many fields from medicine
to finance to entertainment. Or many experts predict that AI will play an increasingly
important role in shaping the future of society and the economy. So AI is really going to affect
society. And our last sentence, the use of AI has raised many ethical questions, especially as a
writing teacher. I have to have lots of conversations with my students about using AI,
when it is appropriate and when it's not. Number two, revolutionary. Revolutionary has a lot of
useful word forms. We have revolutionary, which is an adjective, revolution, which is a noun,
and to revolutionize, which is the verb. And all of these words have a meaning of
causing a huge change, usually politically or technologically. For example, the French
Revolution of 1789 was a period of radical political and social change that ended the
monarchy and encouraged democratic ideals. Or the civil rights movement in the 1950s
led to many revolutionary changes in society. So these last two sentences, there were a lot
of changes politically and socially. Next, the Industrial Revolution was a period of revolutionary
change that transformed society and the economy. Now, the Industrial Revolution was when all of
these new technologies were being developed, and that led to revolutionary change. And lastly,
the invention of the smartphone was a revolutionary development that changed the way we communicate
and access information. Number three, clash. This word can be used as a noun or a verb,
and it means a fight, disagreement, or a conflict. And this word is interesting because it can be
used in a lot of different situations, maybe personally, like a clash between two people.
It could be used in terms of war, like a clash between countries. Or I even see this word used
with time, like a time conflict. So let's look at a few examples. Democrats and Republicans often
cannot compromise because their beliefs and values clash. Their beliefs disagree. They conflict.
They are opposite. Or the clash between the police and the protesters resulted in several
injuries and arrests. Notice here how we're using the word clash as a noun this time. And lastly,
you might say, I'm sorry, I can't go to the meeting. I have a doctor's appointment that day,
and the times clash. That means that the time of your meeting and the doctor's appointment
conflict. They're happening at the same time, so they clash. And therefore, you can't go to
the meeting. Okay, let's read about AI in journalism. It says, recently, AI tools such as
ChatGPT and BARD have been in the spotlight. With the most recent release of ChatGPT,
people are being humbled by its abilities to generate human-sounding language and easily
complete a variety of tasks. And in this section, we're going to review the words spotlight, humble,
and generate. Now, with the word spotlight, we have a common phrase, which is in the spotlight,
and spotlight is being used as a noun. Or you can use spotlight as a verb. And this means to get a
lot of attention or to highlight something. So as you can see in the pictures, these are all theater
spotlights. And this is to highlight a character on stage. So when we use this outside of theater,
we're talking about giving someone or something a lot of attention. It could be good attention,
or it could be bad attention. But a lot of people are talking about this person and giving them
attention. So to put it in a sentence, we can say, the company's CEO was in the spotlight
after he was found stealing money from the company. So here, the CEO is in the spotlight.
He's getting a lot of bad attention. Number two, the celebrity's personal life was constantly
in the spotlight with paparazzi following her everywhere. So in this case, the celebrity is
getting a lot of attention, neither good nor bad, but the paparazzi is following her and she is in
the spotlight. Or you could say, in this presentation, I want to spotlight the various
uses of AI. You want to highlight or you want to feature them. And lastly, the charity Feeding
America spotlights the problem of hunger in the US. And in the reading, we see that AI tools like
ChatGPT and BARD have been in the spotlight. Everyone has been talking about them lately.
And next we see the word humble. Now the word humble can be used as an adjective to be humble,
or it can also be used as a verb to humble someone. And we also often see the verb used
in the passive voice, be humbled by. So the adjective means not arrogant. You don't think
you're more important than other people. And when we see this used as a verb, we can say to humble
someone. This means to make someone feel less important or less proud, usually because of a
challenging experience, or because something or someone better comes along that is more important
or better than them. Basically, it just means to make someone humble. Let's take a look at a few
examples. The experience of living in a foreign country humbled him and made him think about his
own culture more. So after living in a new country, this person had several experiences that made him
think about his own culture and maybe some of the downsides to it. So in this case, he was humbled
because he learned more about a new culture and how other people do things. Or another example,
after losing the election by many points, the politician was humbled and vowed to work harder
in the future. So in this situation, the politician did very poorly in his election.
He lost by a lot, which made him realize that he wasn't as good as he thought he was. It humbled
him. It made him feel less important or less good at what he does. Or another example, the CEO was
humbled by the company's financial struggles, and he realized he was the one to blame. In this
situation, the company's CEO realizes that he's not as good or not as important as he thought he
was. The financial struggles humbled him. He was humbled. And our next word, to generate. To
generate means to produce or create something, often using a specific method. For example,
the wind turbines generate electricity by using the power of the wind. The turbines are creating,
producing, or generating energy. Or the marketing team worked to generate interest in the new
product through social media and commercials. They want to produce or create interest. Or Google
generates most of its revenue through advertising. We often see this collocation generate revenue,
which means to create or produce a profit, basically to make money. And in our reading,
we see that AI can create, produce, or generate human-sounding language. And this can be a very
humbling experience. So we can say that AI is humbling people, or people are being humbled
by AI's capabilities. They're starting to think that the computers might be better at writing or
speaking or using human language than people. And before we move on in our reading, I just want to
remind you that if you have found these words helpful so far, make sure you like this video,
subscribe to my channel, and download the free PDF activities that come with this video,
so you can really practice and use these words on your own. Moving on. From writing emails to
learning a language, the applications for these tools are endless, as you can access vast amounts
of information that would have been impossible to gather and process manually. However, like any
other innovation, AI also raises ethical and social questions, especially in the field of journalism.
So we see here the word application, and this word also has several different word forms that I want
to highlight. We have two noun forms, application and app. App is short for like a mobile application.
Then we also have the verb to apply, and I know you're probably familiar with the word application,
meaning a form or a paper to fill out, like a job application. But this word actually has a more
advanced meaning, which is how something can be applied or used. Basically, it means the purpose
of a tool. So you can think about application meaning a purpose or the use of something. Let's
look at a few examples. Our research findings on how to learn new vocabulary have many practical
applications for language teachers. So the research findings about vocabulary apply, they relate to,
they can help language teachers. There are many practical applications or ways of using the results
or purposes of the results. Basically, ways that the findings can apply to the classroom and
So I've used it as a noun, and we can also use it as a verb. So we can say,
our research findings on how to learn new vocabulary apply to language learners and
teachers. So these two sentences have similar meanings, we've just used different word forms
of the same word, apply. You can also say, drones have a wide range of applications,
from aerial photography, to responding to emergencies, to delivering packages. So here,
we see that there are many uses, purposes, or applications for using drones. And lastly,
the new habit tracker app has many applications, including monitoring your exercise and checking
your sleep habits. Now this sentence is kind of funny because we use app to mean a mobile
application. And then we say that this app has many applications, meaning purposes or many uses.
Now we probably wouldn't really write this sentence because it sounds quite repetitive.
But I want to show you how you can use each form of the word app or application. And I think it's
kind of a funny example to show you. And next we see the word endless, which means having no ending,
no limit, no boundary, basically just infinite. And to use this in a sentence, we can say,
as far as we know, the universe is considered endless. It never ends. Or, unfortunately,
our budget isn't endless. So we need to cut some programs. We do not have endless money. Or,
the winner of the company's sweepstakes won an endless supply of fried chicken for an entire
year. Now I always see the phrase endless supply when I hear about different sweepstakes being
offered at various companies. So endless supply is definitely a good collocation to know. And in
our reading, we see that the applications or the purposes or uses for AI tools are endless. There
are an infinite number of things we can do with these AI tools. Then we see the word manually.
And manually has two word forms. We have the adjective form, manual, and the adverb form,
manually. And these words mean to do something by hand or with your own mind, not using machines
or computers. For example, the intern had to manually organize thousands of files in the
storage room. They had to take each file one by one with their hand and organize them. The chef
manually kneaded the dough to create the perfect texture for the bread. To knead means to do this
motion with your hands when you're making bread. And the chef did that manually or by hand,
opposed to using an electric mixer. The researcher manually calculated each equation just to double
check the answer from the computer. So instead of using a calculator, he did it by hand,
he did it manually. And lastly, in the age of technology, some people still prefer
manual labor, such as woodworking or construction. The phrase manual labor is a great collocation to
know. And it really includes any job that is more physical than mental. So a lot of office jobs or
jobs you do with a computer are not really manual labor. But jobs like farming, housekeeping,
and construction are all considered manual labor. And our next word is innovation. Again,
we have lots of different word forms here. We have the noun innovation, the verb to innovate,
and the adjective innovative, innovative, innovative. That O in the adjective reduces to schwa,
it pretty much gets left out, innovative. And all of these words relate to the idea of
creating new ideas, methods or technologies that improve or transform current ones. For example,
the internet was the most important innovation of the 20th century. Or Apple is considered one of
the most innovative companies in the world. Or the company's success was due to its innovative
approaches and subsequent innovations in medicine and technology. Or the organization encourages
its employees to think outside the box and innovate, seeking out new solutions and approaches
to problems. And in our reading, we are talking about the innovation of AI chatbots like ChatGPT
and BARD, which can generate human language. So let's keep reading. One of the most controversial
aspects of AI in journalism is its impact on employment. Some people fear that AI will replace
human writers, editors and other professionals. Some argue that eventually writers and journalists
will become disposable, thus reducing the number of jobs. However, others argue that AI can
compensate for this loss by correcting human errors and freeing up more time for investigating
and generating ideas and reporting. And our next word is disposable. This word has two common word
the verb form is to dispose. And the preposition that goes with this word is of, to dispose of,
to dispose of. And the verb form means to throw away something or get rid of something. Then we
have the adjective form disposable. And the adjective means that it's able to be disposed of,
it's able to be thrown away. When referring to physical products, this means that something is
to be used only once and then thrown out, like disposable Tupperware or disposable plates, etc.
When we're not talking about physical products, and we're talking about people or jobs or employment,
it means that someone is not useful or can be easily thrown away, or in other words, easily
fired. So let's take a look at all of these in some sentences. Please dispose of your trash in
bins to keep the area clean. This is just a fancy way of saying please throw away your trash in the
bin. Please dispose of your trash. Or the company disposed of their old equipment to make room for
new machines. They disposed of their old equipment. Or the restaurant only uses disposable plates and
cups for takeout orders. Or the hospital staff wears disposable gloves and masks for hygiene
purposes. So in these examples, the plates and the gloves and the masks could be thrown away after
one use. They are disposable. Now talking about employment, we can say many low wage workers are
often treated as disposable by their employers, who may replace them easily without much thought
or concern. So if you're at your job, and you do not feel valued, you might feel disposable, like
they can just let you go and it doesn't matter. And another one, fast food workers are afraid that
electronic kiosks, which can take your order for you, will make their jobs disposable. And in our
reading, we can see that some people are afraid that AI chat tools will be able to replace humans,
thus making journalists jobs disposable. But then we see a positive aspect. However, others argue
that AI can compensate for this loss by correcting human errors and freeing up more time for
investigating, generating ideas and reporting. And our next word is compensate. Again, this has several
different word forms. And there are a couple different meanings. So let's go through them.
To compensate as a verb means to make up for something that was lost. It can also just mean
to pay someone. Then we see the word overcompensate. And this means trying too hard to correct something,
which usually results in a new problem or imbalance. And lastly, we see the noun form,
which is compensation. And this means a payment for something. But know that it doesn't always
have to be money. It could also be something for free. So let's take a look at a few examples.
To compensate ticket buyers for the cancelled concert, everyone was given an extra ticket
for the new show. So here we can see something was lost, we have a cancelled show.
But to make up for what was lost or to compensate for that cancelled show, everyone received a free
ticket. Or the insurance company will compensate the car owner for the damage caused by the accident.
So the insurance company is going to compensate the car owner, they are going to pay the car owner.
What was lost, or in this case, damaged in the accident. And in our next two sentences,
we see the word overcompensate, which again, means trying too hard to correct something.
So we see, Adrian feels insecure about her appearance. So she tries to overcompensate
by working hard and succeeding in her career. So here, Adrian feels bad in one area of her life.
And to make up for it, to compensate for it, she goes too extreme in the other direction.
She works too much, she's trying to succeed too much, because she is insecure in another area of
her life. Another example, divorced parents may feel guilty for breaking up their child's home,
and thus overcompensate by doing whatever the child wants. So in this case, the parents are
trying to correct the situation of their divorce by letting their child do whatever they want.
They are overcompensating, they are going too far to make up for what was lost. And lastly,
the company will compensate employees for any overtime work they do. To compensate really can
just mean to pay someone. And so in our reading, some people argue that even if we lose some
journalists, or even if we lose some jobs, AI will make up for this loss or will compensate us by
correcting journalists errors and saving them time. And moving on, it says, for the most part, AI and
journalism will simply become a tool, much like that of a calculator, to make writers more efficient
and able to focus on the most important aspects of their job. It is unlikely that AI will completely
get rid of human writers, just as it is unlikely that human writers will completely ignore AI.
Instead, we are likely to see a ubiquitous and complementary use of AI tools, such as natural
language processing, data mining, and automated fact checking in various stages of the journalistic
process. Overall, one thing is for certain, whether you agree or disagree with its purpose or use,
AI is here to stay. And here we see the phrase for the most part and the adjectives
ubiquitous and complementary. So let's take a look at these words. For the most part is a phrase and
it is used just like an adverb. This phrase means generally, mostly, or usually. And in a sentence,
we can say, the project was successful for the most part, except for a few minor issues. So the
project was mostly or generally successful overall. Or we can say, the book was well written for the
most part, but the ending was disappointing. So again, for the most part, mostly, generally, the
book was well written, just the ending wasn't too great. Or for the most part, New York City is
incredible. The only big problems are how dirty and expensive it is. So mostly, generally, usually,
New York City is incredible. Or lastly, I eat healthy for the most part, but on weekends,
I indulge a bit more. So in this example, for the most part means generally, mostly, or usually. And
in our reading, it says, for the most part, or generally, or mostly, AI will just become a tool
that helps journalists write. It's not going to take over their jobs, but it will mainly,
mostly, generally, just be used as a tool. And ubiquitous is one of my favorite adjectives,
and it means to be found everywhere or just be super common. For example, smartphones have become
ubiquitous in modern society. Everybody has a smartphone these days. Or Starbucks is ubiquitous,
even in cities outside of the United States. Or English has become the ubiquitous language
of business and commerce. Or electric cars are becoming more and more ubiquitous as gas prices
rise. They're everywhere. And in our reading, they say that the use of these AI tools is going
to be ubiquitous. It's going to be everywhere. And lastly, we see the word complimentary.
This is the adjective form, and it comes from the verb to compliment. Now, you might be thinking
that to compliment means to say something nice to someone, like to compliment their shirt or their
shoes. But that word is actually spelled with an I. It just sounds exactly the same as this verb,
to compliment. But this word means to work well together by creating a balance, combining
strengths, or just making something more beautiful. Let's see how we use it in some sentences.
The sweet and salty flavors of the salted caramel compliment each other perfectly. So the sweet and
the salt, they work well together. They make each other more flavorful. They compliment each other.
Or you can say, that color compliments your eyes nicely. That means that the color of their shirt
makes their eyes more beautiful. Or the new assistant has complimentary skills to the manager,
which will enhance the team's performance. So in this case, maybe the manager is good at some
things that the assistant isn't, but the assistant is good at things that the manager isn't. So their
skills compliment each other. They work well together. They create a balance. And similarly,
his outgoing personality is complimentary to her shyness, making them a great match.
And our last example, they compliment each other well because their strengths compensate for each
other's weaknesses. Their strengths make up for each other's weaknesses. And in our reading,
we see that the strengths of AI, like fact-checking and finding grammar errors,
can compliment a journalist's job and all of the things that people are good at. So we will see AI
used in complimentary ways. Now, don't forget to download the free PDF to practice all of these
wonderful words. And also, please let me know what you think of it. Again, this is a sample
from my ebook, so I would love to hear your thoughts and how to make it better. So thank
you so much for watching this video. I hope you learned something new. And go practice!
I'll see you next time. Bye!
